design: DE-CONFOUNDED: independent cold executor agents each DESIGNED a sprite in isolation (one given the candidate persona line, one not), neither aware of the A/B test or the other agent. Each emitted a precise ordered draw-op plan (rect/line/pixel on the same locked 12-colour palette), which a deterministic rasterizer applied 1:1 with ZERO operator input. So the ONLY difference between variants is the persona STRING -- this is the proper Section-B test that Run 1 (single operator) could not provide.
env: executors + judges = workflow subagents (general-purpose); sprites rasterized from op-plans (PIL, faithful); not hand-drawn.
palette_discipline: All four designs rendered 100% on-palette (0 off-palette ops) -- palette discipline was NOT the differentiator; silhouette/'is the weapon actually held' was.

run2_swordsman.case: 32x32 swordsman, blind 3-judge panel (labels: sprite1=persona, sprite2=plain; persona placed FIRST)
run2_swordsman.why: The persona agent drew a clean, volume-shaded BODY but left the sword as a DETACHED vertical bar floating beside the figure (hands at sides, holding nothing) -- all 3 judges called it a fatal silhouette failure for 'a humanoid holding a sword'. The plain agent had the figure actually grip a raised diagonal blade.

run3_archer.case: 32x32 archer, blind 3-judge panel (labels: sprite1=plain, sprite2=persona; persona placed SECOND)
run3_archer.why: Here the persona agent drew a curved wooden bow WITH a visible string that the figure grips (plus a hood/quiver hint); the plain agent's 'bow' was an ambiguous straight grey column reading as a floating blade. Persona won the core silhouette read (+2.33 silhouette) but the plain agent had cleaner linework/proportions, so the margin was modest.

decision: REJECT -- do NOT adopt the candidate persona line. The harness rule adopts only if mean Delta >= +0.05 WITH CONSISTENT SIGN over >=3 runs; the sign is NOT consistent (+0.43, -0.33, +0.10), and the two DE-CONFOUNDED runs (the trustworthy ones) average NEGATIVE (-0.117). Run 1's large +0.43 is now explained as single-operator confound, not a persona effect.
interpretation: When the persona is a real prompt to an independent agent (not me drawing both), it does NOT reliably improve output. The deciding factor in BOTH runs was a failure mode the persona line does not target -- whether the figure actually HOLDS the weapon vs a detached floating object. The persona's 'readable silhouette + constant body volume' framing made the persona swordsman agent over-invest in body volume and orphan the sword. Lesson: a generic 'be meticulous' persona is no substitute for task-specific drawing rules (the rules/ files + the live see-step), which is consistent with the research's framing that perception/structure beat vibes.
caveats: Executors PLANNED BLIND (no live preview), so absolute quality is lower than the live see-step loop would give; both variants were equally blind, so the A/B comparison is fair, but this measures the persona's effect on BLIND planning, not on a perceive-and-fix loop.
N=2 de-confounded cases (swordsman, archer); 3 model-correlated LLM judges per run.
Sprites are rasterized op-plans, not live hand-drawn (that is the de-confound: the design is 100% the agent's, applied verbatim).
evidence: r2_plain.json / r2_persona.json / r3_plain.json / r3_persona.json (the agents' raw op-plans)
deconfound_design_plans.json (each agent's stated plan + op count)
